chunks.text: Wheat is a Rabi season crop grown in Uttar Pradesh, best suited to loamy or clay loam soils with good drainage. It thrives in temperatures between 10-25°C and requires around 750 mm of rainfall. Recommended varieties include HD 2967, PBW 343, K 1006, and DBW 187, with a yield potential of up to 45 quintals per hectare. Common diseases are Rust (controlled by Propiconazole spray), Loose Smut (controlled by seed treatment with Carbendazim), and Powdery Mildew (controlled by Sulphur 80% WP). Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 120 kg, DAP 50 kg, MOP 40 kg, Zinc Sulphate 25 kg. Apply DAP and Zinc Sulphate at sowing, 60 kg Urea after first irrigation (CRI), and another 60 kg Urea after second irrigation. Sowing window is 15 October to 15 November; harvesting occurs in March-April. Early sowing improves tillering and grain filling, while late sowing risks heat stress. Critical irrigation stages include CRI (20-25 DAS)

chunks.text: Rice is a major Kharif crop in Eastern Uttar Pradesh and the Terai belt, thriving in clayey and alluvial soils with 150-300 cm rainfall and temperatures between 20-35°C. Recommended varieties like Swarna, IR-64, and MTU-7029 can yield up to 50 quintals per hectare. Key diseases include Blast, Bacterial Leaf Blight, and Sheath Blight, controlled using Tricyclazole, Copper oxychloride, and Carbendazim respectively. Fertilizer per hectare includes 100 kg Urea, 50 kg DAP, 50 kg MOP, and 25 kg Zinc Sulphate, applied in three stages: basal, tillering, and panicle initiation. Nursery sowing begins in June, transplanting in July, and harvesting in October-November. Maintain 5-10 cm standing water during growth and drain before harvest. Use Butachlor @ 1.5 l/ha for weed control. Harvest when grains turn golden and dry to 12-14% moisture. MSP is around ₹2,100/quintal with strong mandi demand. Farmers benefit from seed subsidies and PMFBY insurance. Crop rotation with wheat or pulses improves soil health. Best practices include soil testing every 2-3 years, certified seed use, and weekly pest scouting. Checklist: test soil, prepare nursery, maintain water depth, control weeds at 20-25 DAS, scout for diseases weekly, drain field 10 days before harvest, and prepare storage. Farmer tips: water depth suppresses weeds, timely transplanting boosts yield, and drying grain ensures better market price.

chunks.text: Potato is a short-duration Rabi crop widely grown in Agra, Farrukhabad, and Kannauj regions of Uttar Pradesh. It prefers sandy loam soils with good drainage, 15-20°C temperature, and 50-75 cm rainfall. Popular varieties include Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Bahar, Kufri Chandramukhi, and Kufri Pukhraj, with yield potential up to 250 quintals per hectare. Major diseases are Late Blight, Early Blight, and Black Scurf, controlled through fungicide sprays and seed treatment. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 120 kg, DAP 100 kg, MOP 100 kg, and FYM 20 tons. Apply half Urea with full FYM, DAP, and MOP at planting, and the rest at earthing up (25-30 DAS). Planting is done in October-November and harvesting in February-March. Early planting improves tuber bulking and reduces blight risk, while late planting risks heat stress. Irrigation is critical after planting, at tuber initiation (20-25 DAS), and during bulking (40-60 DAS). Light irrigation every 7-10 days is ideal; avoid waterlogging. Furrow or drip irrigation is recommended. Common weeds include Chenopodium, Amaranthus, and Cyperus, controlled by hand weeding or Metribuzin

chunks.text: Maize is a major Kharif crop in Eastern Uttar Pradesh, grown in well-drained alluvial and loamy soils with 60-120 cm rainfall and temperatures of 20-30°C. Popular hybrids like HQPM-1, Ganga-5, and Deccan-107 yield up to 50 quintals per hectare. Key diseases include Turcicum Leaf Blight, Downy Mildew, and Rust, managed by seed treatment and timely fungicide sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 120 kg, DAP 60 kg, MOP 40 kg, and Zinc Sulphate 25 kg, applied in three stages—basal, knee-high, and tasseling. Sowing is done in June-July with the onset of monsoon, and harvesting in September-October. Irrigation is critical at knee-high, tasseling, and grain filling stages. Weeds like Cyperus, Amaranthus, and Echinochloa are controlled by hand weeding or Atrazine. Harvest when husks turn brown and grains harden (~20% moisture), then dry to 12% before storage. MSP is around ₹1,900/quintal, with better prices for clean, dried grain. Crop rotation with wheat, potato, or pulses improves soil fertility and breaks pest cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified hybrid seeds, timely irrigation, and record keeping. Farmer tips: irrigate at knee-high and tasseling, weed once early, and dry cobs well before storage.

chunks.text: Barley is an important Rabi crop in Western Uttar Pradesh, grown mainly on sandy loam and light soils with 30-40 cm rainfall and temperatures between 10-25°C. Recommended varieties like RD-2035, RD-2552, and K-551 can yield up to 35 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Leaf Blight, Powdery Mildew, and Smut, which are managed by seed treatment and timely fungicide sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 60 kg, DAP 30 kg, and MOP 20 kg, applied half at sowing and half at tillering. Sowing is done in November and harvesting in March. Early sowing ensures better tillering and higher yield, while late sowing risks poor grain filling due to rising temperatures. Irrigation is critical at tillering and grain filling, with 2-3 irrigations usually sufficient. Weeds like Phalaris minor and Chenopodium are controlled by hand weeding or Isoproturon. Harvest when grains are hard and golden with ~18% moisture, then dry to 12% before storage. MSP is around ₹1,635/quintal, with breweries offering premium prices for clean, bold grains. Crop rotation with pulses or maize improves soil fertility and breaks disease cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified seed use, FYM application, timely irrigation, and record keeping. Farmer tips: sow on time, weed once early, irrigate at tillering, and dry grain well before storage for better price.

chunks.text: Peas are an important Rabi pulse and vegetable crop in Uttar Pradesh, grown mainly in well-drained loamy soils with 40-60 cm rainfall and temperatures between 10-20°C. Popular varieties like Arkel, Azad P-1, Rachna, and Pant P-25 can yield up to 18 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Powdery Mildew, Rust, and Wilt, which are managed by seed treatment and timely fungicide sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 20 kg, DAP 40 kg, and MOP 20 kg, applied as a basal dose at sowing. Sowing is done in October-November and harvesting in February-March. Early sowing ensures better nodulation and pod set, while late sowing risks heat stress. Irrigation is critical at flowering and pod filling, with 2-3 light irrigations usually sufficient. Weeds like Chenopodium, Phalaris, and Amaranthus are controlled by hand weeding or Pendimethalin. Harvest when pods turn greenish-yellow and seeds harden, then dry to 10% moisture before storage. MSP is around ₹4,400/quintal, with premium prices for bold, uniform grains. Crop rotation with wheat, maize, or barley improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation and breaks disease cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified seed use, FYM application, timely irrigation, and record keeping. Farmer tips: sow on time, weed once early, irrigate at flowering, and harvest when pods turn yellow for better yield and price.

chunks.text: Groundnut is an important Kharif oilseed crop in Bundelkhand and Eastern Uttar Pradesh. It grows best in sandy loam and light soils with 50-100 cm rainfall and temperatures between 20-30°C. Popular varieties like JL-24, ICGV-91114, Girnar-2, and TG-37A can yield up to 20 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Tikka Leaf Spot, Rust, and Collar Rot, which are managed by seed treatment and timely fungicide sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 20 kg, DAP 40 kg, MOP 40 kg, and Gypsum 250 kg, applied half at sowing and half at flowering. Sowing is done in June-July with the onset of monsoon, and harvesting in October. Irrigation is critical at flowering, pegging, and pod development, with 4-5 irrigations needed if rainfall is low. Weeds like Cyperus, Amaranthus, and Echinochloa are controlled by hand weeding or Pendimethalin. Harvest when leaves turn yellow and pods mature with darkened shells, then dry pods to 8-10% moisture before storage. MSP is around ₹5,900/quintal, with bold, uniform kernels fetching premium prices. Crop rotation with wheat, barley, or pulses improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation and breaks disease cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified seed use, gypsum application, timely irrigation, and record keeping. Farmer tips: sow early with monsoon, weed once early, irrigate at pegging, and harvest when pods mature for better yield and price.

chunks.text: Cotton is a key Kharif cash crop grown in limited pockets of Western Uttar Pradesh. It thrives in black cotton and loamy soils with 60-100 cm rainfall and temperatures between 25-35°C. Popular varieties include H-777, F-846, RCH-134 Bt, and NCS-145 Bt, with yield potential up to 20 quintals per hectare. Major diseases are Wilt, Leaf Curl Virus, and Boll Rot, managed by seed treatment, resistant varieties, and timely sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 100 kg, DAP 50 kg, and MOP 50 kg, applied in three stages—basal, square formation, and boll development. Sowing is done in April-May and harvesting in October-November. Early sowing ensures better boll setting and reduced pest pressure, while late sowing increases bollworm and whitefly risk. Irrigation is critical at square formation, flowering, and boll development, with 6-8 irrigations usually required. Weeds like Cyperus, Amaranthus, and Echinochloa are controlled by hand weeding or Pendimethalin. Harvest when bolls open fully and fluff is visible, picking in 3-4 rounds for clean lint. MSP is around ₹6,620/quintal, with premium prices for clean, white lint. Crop rotation with wheat or pulses improves soil fertility and breaks pest cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified Bt seed use, FYM application, timely irrigation, and record keeping. Farmer tips: sow early, irrigate at flowering, and pick cotton clean and dry for better price.

chunks.text: Bajra (Pearl Millet) is a hardy Kharif crop grown widely in Western Uttar Pradesh and Bundelkhand. It thrives in sandy and light soils with 40-65 cm rainfall and temperatures between 25-35°C. Popular varieties like HHB-67 Improved, ICTP-8203, Pusa-322, and RHB-177 can yield up to 20 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Downy Mildew, Ergot, and Smut, which are managed by seed treatment, resistant varieties, and timely fungicide sprays. Fertilizer per hectare: Urea 60 kg, DAP 30 kg, and MOP 20 kg, applied half at sowing and half at knee-high stage. Sowing is done in June-July with the onset of monsoon, and harvesting in September-October. Early sowing ensures better tillering and grain filling, while late sowing risks moisture stress. Irrigation is usually not required, but 1-2 irrigations at tillering, flowering, and grain filling can boost yield in dry spells. Weeds like Cyperus, Amaranthus, and Echinochloa are controlled by hand weeding or Atrazine. Harvest when grains are hard and panicles dry, then dry to 12% moisture before storage. MSP is around ₹2,350/quintal, with clean, bold grains fetching better prices. Crop rotation with wheat, mustard, or pulses improves soil fertility and breaks pest cycles. Best practices include soil testing, certified seed use, FYM application, timely irrigation if needed, and record keeping. Farmer tips: sow early with monsoon, weed once early, irrigate at flowering if no rain, and harvest when panicles dry for better yield and price.

chunks.text: Onion is an important Rabi and Kharif crop in Uttar Pradesh, mainly grown in Farrukhabad and Etawah. It prefers well-drained sandy loam soils, moderate rainfall of 40-60 cm, and cool temperatures of 13-25°C. Popular varieties like N-53, Agrifound Light Red, Bhima Super, and Pusa Red can yield 250-300 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Purple Blotch, Stemphylium Blight, and Downy Mildew, managed by seed treatment and fungicide sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 100:50:50 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, with half nitrogen applied at sowing and the rest 30-40 days later. Nursery is prepared in mid-September, transplanting is done after 30-40 days, and harvest occurs in March-April when necks soften and tops fall. Irrigation is light but regular every 7-10 days, stopped 10-15 days before harvest. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or Pendimethalin. Harvested bulbs are sun-dried for 4-5 days and stored in cool, ventilated spaces. Onion has high year-round demand, with seasonal price fluctuations and value addition options like flakes and pickles. Farmer tips: prepare nursery on time, apply FYM, monitor for purple blotch, and stop irrigation before harvest for better storage and price.

chunks.text: Tomato is a major vegetable crop in Uttar Pradesh, grown in districts like Varanasi, Mirzapur, Kanpur, and Lucknow during both Rabi and Kharif seasons. It thrives in well-drained loamy soils with 60-80 cm rainfall and temperatures between 18-27°C. Popular varieties such as Pusa Ruby, Pusa Hybrid-1, Arka Vikas, Naveen, and Kashi Vishesh can yield 300-500 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Early Blight, Late Blight, and Leaf Curl Virus, which are managed by resistant varieties, seed treatment, and timely fungicide/insecticide sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 100:50:50 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, applied in split doses at transplanting and flowering. Nursery is prepared in June for Kharif and September for Rabi, with transplanting after 25-30 days. Harvest begins 60-70 days after transplanting and continues for 2-3 months. Irrigation is required every 6-8 days, avoiding waterlogging during flowering and fruiting. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. Fruits are harvested at breaker stage for transport and fully ripe for local markets, then stored at 12-15°C for 1-2 weeks. Tomato has high demand year-round for fresh use and processing into ketchup, sauce, and soup. MSP is not fixed, but prices peak in lean months. Crop rotation with onion, cabbage, or okra improves soil health and reduces pest cycles. Best practices include staking plants, using disease-resistant hybrids, and adopting IPM. Farmer tips: prepare nursery on time, apply FYM, irrigate regularly, monitor for blight and leaf curl, and harvest at breaker stage for better market returns.

chunks.text: Brinjal (Eggplant) is a widely grown vegetable crop in Uttar Pradesh, especially in Varanasi, Allahabad, Kanpur, and Lucknow. It thrives in sandy loam and clay loam soils with 60-80 cm rainfall and temperatures between 20-30°C. Popular varieties like Pusa Purple Long, Pusa Kranti, Arka Nidhi, Kashi Taru, and Pant Samrat can yield 250-400 quintals per hectare. Major problems include Wilt, Shoot and Fruit Borer, and Little Leaf disease, which are managed by resistant varieties, crop rotation, pheromone traps, and timely sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 100:50:50 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, applied in split doses at transplanting and flowering. Nursery is prepared in May for Kharif and September for Rabi, with transplanting after 25-30 days. Harvest begins 60-70 days after transplanting and continues for 2-3 months. Irrigation is required every 7-10 days, avoiding waterlogging during flowering and fruiting. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. Fruits are harvested at tender stage with glossy skin and firm texture, and stored at 10-12°C for 7-10 days. Brinjal has high demand for fresh use and processing into pickles, dehydrated slices, and ready-to-cook packs. Crop rotation with wheat, okra, or cabbage improves soil health and reduces pest incidence. Best practices include using resistant hybrids, IPM, and timely irrigation. Farmer tips: prepare nursery on time, apply FYM, monitor for shoot and fruit borer with pheromone traps, and harvest fruits at tender stage for better market price.

chunks.text: Cabbage is a major winter vegetable crop in Uttar Pradesh, grown in districts like Varanasi, Gorakhpur, Lucknow, and Kanpur. It thrives in loamy soils with good moisture, cool temperatures of 15-20°C, and 50-75 cm rainfall. Popular varieties such as Golden Acre, Pusa Mukta, Pusa Drumhead, Kashi Manohar, and Pant Gobhi-2 can yield 250-350 quintals per hectare. Major diseases include Black Rot, Downy Mildew, and Club Root, which are managed by seed treatment, resistant varieties, and fungicide sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 120:60:60 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, applied in split doses at transplanting and head formation. Nursery is prepared in August-September, transplanting is done after 25-30 days, and harvest occurs in January-February when compact, firm heads form. Irrigation is required every 7-10 days, especially during head formation. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. Harvested heads are cut with a few wrapper leaves and stored at 0-5°C for 2-3 weeks. Cabbage has high demand in winter for fresh use and processing into pickles, salads, and frozen shreds. Crop rotation with potato, pea, or wheat improves soil fertility and reduces disease risk. Best practices include using certified seeds of staggered varieties, maintaining cool moist conditions, and adopting crop rotation. Farmer tips: prepare nursery on time, apply FYM, irrigate regularly, monitor for black rot and downy mildew, and harvest compact heads for better price.

chunks.text: Carrot is an important Rabi root crop in Uttar Pradesh, mainly grown in Meerut, Aligarh, Varanasi, and Kanpur. It prefers sandy loam soils rich in organic matter, cool temperatures of 15-20°C, and 50-75 cm rainfall. Popular varieties like Pusa Rudhira, Pusa Kesar, Nantes, Pusa Meghali, and Kashi Harit can yield 250-300 quintals per hectare. Major problems include Alternaria Leaf Blight and Root Knot Nematode, which are managed by crop rotation, organic amendments, and resistant varieties. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 80:40:40 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, applied in split doses at sowing and 30-40 days later. Sowing is done in October-November by direct seeding, and harvesting occurs in January-February when roots are bright, tender, and marketable. Irrigation is required every 8-10 days, especially during root development. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. Roots are carefully uprooted and stored at 0-4°C for 2-3 months. Carrot has high demand in winter for fresh use and processing into juices, pickles, frozen cubes, and sweets like gajar ka halwa. Crop rotation with wheat, pea, or onion improves soil fertility and reduces nematode infestation. Best practices include timely sowing, thinning seedlings at 20 days, and using organic amendments. Farmer tips: sow in October-November, thin seedlings after 20 days, irrigate regularly, and harvest roots at tender stage for better price.

chunks.text: Turmeric is an important spice crop in Eastern Uttar Pradesh, mainly grown in Varanasi, Ghazipur, Jaunpur, and Azamgarh. It thrives in loamy soils rich in organic matter, warm humid climates with 100-150 cm rainfall, and temperatures of 20-30°C. Popular varieties like Suguna, Sudarsana, Rajendra Sonia, Krishna, and Roma can yield 200-250 quintals of fresh rhizomes per hectare. Major diseases include Leaf Blotch, Rhizome Rot, and Leaf Spot, which are managed by disease-free planting material, crop rotation, mulching, and fungicide sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 100:50:50 N:P:K plus 25 tons FYM, applied in split doses at planting and during crop growth. Planting is done in May-June using rhizomes, and harvesting occurs in January-February when leaves turn yellow and dry. Irrigation is needed every 10-12 days, with mulching to conserve moisture and suppress weeds. After harvest, rhizomes are boiled, dried, and stored in cool, ventilated places. Turmeric has high demand as a spice, medicine, and dye, with value addition options like turmeric powder, extracts, and oil. Crop rotation with wheat, pulses, or vegetables improves soil health and reduces disease incidence. Farmer tips: plant rhizomes with FYM in May-June, irrigate regularly, mulch fields, monitor for leaf blotch and rhizome rot, and harvest after leaves dry for better yield and quality.

chunks.text: Chili is an important spice and vegetable crop in Uttar Pradesh, mainly grown in Varanasi, Mirzapur, Jaunpur, Kanpur, and Lucknow. It thrives in well-drained loamy soils, warm humid climates with 60-80 cm rainfall, and temperatures of 20-30°C. Popular varieties like Pusa Jwala, Pant C-1, Kashi Anmol, Kashi Surkh, and G-4 can yield 100-150 quintals of green fruits or 25-30 quintals of dry fruits per hectare. Major diseases include Leaf Curl Virus, Anthracnose, and Wilt, which are managed by resistant varieties, seed treatment, and integrated pest and vector management. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is 100:50:50 N:P:K plus 20 tons FYM, applied in split doses at transplanting and flowering. Nursery is prepared in May-June, transplanting is done after 25-30 days, and harvest begins in October-November for green fruits and December-January for dry fruits. Irrigation is required every 7-10 days, avoiding waterlogging. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. Green chilies are harvested regularly, while red ripe fruits are dried under the sun and stored in moisture-free bags. Chili has high demand for fresh, dried, and processed products like powder, pickles, sauces, and oleoresin. Crop rotation with onion, tomato, or pulses improves soil health and reduces pest cycles. Best practices include timely nursery preparation, FYM application, resistant varieties, and regular monitoring. Farmer tips: prepare nursery in May-June, transplant after 25-30 days, irrigate every 7-10 days, monitor for leaf curl and anthracnose, and harvest fruits regularly for better yield and income.

chunks.text: Garlic is an important bulb spice crop in Uttar Pradesh, mainly grown in Farrukhabad, Etawah, and Mainpuri. It prefers sandy loam to clay loam soils rich in organic matter, cool weather during growth, and warm, dry conditions at maturity. Popular varieties like Yamuna Safed-3, Agrifound White, and G-50 can yield 100-120 quintals of dry bulbs per hectare. Major diseases include Purple Blotch, Stemphylium Blight, and Basal Rot, which are managed by disease-free cloves, crop rotation, and fungicide sprays. Fertilizer recommendation per hectare is Urea 220 kg, DAP 110 kg, MOP 80 kg, and 20-25 tons FYM, applied in split doses. Planting is done in October-November, and harvesting in March-April when leaves turn yellow and bulbs mature. Irrigation is required every 10-12 days, but should be stopped 2-3 weeks before harvest. Weeds are controlled by hand weeding or mulching. After harvest, bulbs are dried in shade and stored in ventilated rooms. Garlic has high demand as a spice, condiment, and medicinal crop, with value addition options like powder, paste, and extracts. Crop rotation with wheat, pulses, or vegetables improves soil health and reduces disease incidence. Farmer tips: use disease-free cloves, irrigate regularly, stop irrigation before harvest, and dry bulbs properly for better storage and price.

chunks.explain_like_a_farmer: आम के पेड़ को अच्छी धूप और पानी चाहिए। सही किस्म लगाओ जैसे दशहरी या लंगड़ा। फूल आने पर दवा छिड़कना ज़रूरी है ताकि बीमारी न लगे। जून-जुलाई में फल तोड़ो और ठंडी जगह रखो। आम की खेती से अच्छा मुनाफा मिलता है।
